review.note: AI-drafted first pass - SME review required before production

title: Independent Africa: Decolonisation, Case Studies and the Balance Sheet
subject: History

sources.title: The fight against colonialism and imperialism in Africa - Grade 11
Republic of Congo (Democratic Republic of the Congo)
The Arusha Declaration: Tanzania's new revolution
References - Grade 12, South African History Online

slides.title: Independent Africa
slides.lead: Decolonisation, two contrasting case studies (the Congo and Tanzania), and weighing the successes and challenges of independence.

media.alt: A stylised map of the African continent with a rising sun behind it, symbolising a new era of independence.

slides.notes: Frame the unit's central question: after the European empires withdrew, why did some newly independent African states stabilise while others slid into crisis? Define **decolonisation** as the process by which colonies won political independence, mostly between the mid-1950s and mid-1970s. Preview the comparative method: the Congo and Tanzania both became independent in the early 1960s but followed very different paths.

slides.title: The wind of change
slides.lead: In roughly two decades, most of Africa moved from colonial rule to independence.

blocks.text: After the Second World War, European powers were weakened and anti-colonial movements grew stronger. **Ghana** became the first sub-Saharan colony to win independence in 1957 under Kwame Nkrumah. British Prime Minister Harold Macmillan captured the mood in his 1960 'wind of change' speech, acknowledging that African nationalism could not be resisted.

items: 1957: Ghana - the first sub-Saharan colony to gain independence.
1960: the 'Year of Africa', when 17 states became independent.
Independence came through negotiation in some places and armed struggle in others.

slides.notes: Stress continuity and change. Nationalist ideas were not new, but post-war conditions - the cost of empire, the example of India (1947), the United Nations Charter, and a rising educated African elite - made independence achievable. Ask learners to distinguish between *formal* political independence and the deeper economic dependence that often continued afterwards (a theme the case studies return to).

slides.title: The ideas behind the new nations
slides.lead: Four currents of thought shaped how leaders imagined the independent African state.

blocks.heading: Pan-Africanism
blocks.text: The belief that Africans share a common destiny and should unite - expressed in the Organisation of African Unity (OAU), founded in 1963.

blocks.heading: African nationalism
blocks.text: Mass parties mobilised people around the demand for self-rule and the end of foreign domination.

blocks.heading: African socialism
blocks.text: Building development on communal traditions and self-reliance rather than copying Western capitalism.

blocks.heading: The Cold War
blocks.text: The rivalry between the USA and USSR pulled young states into a global contest and shaped, and sometimes destabilised, their politics.

slides.notes: These four ideas give learners an analytical toolkit for the case studies. Pan-Africanism and nationalism explain the *drive* for independence; African socialism and the Cold War explain the *choices and pressures* that followed. Preview: Tanzania is our clearest example of African socialism in practice, while the Congo shows the destructive force of Cold War interference. [SME] Confirm the level of detail on Pan-Africanism/OAU expected by the current CAPS content sequence.

slides.title: Africa becomes independent

events.label: Tanganyika and Zanzibar unite as Tanzania
Mobutu seizes power in the Congo
Arusha Declaration sets out Tanzanian socialism

slides.notes: Use this timeline as an orientation map before the deep dives. Point out that the Congo and Tanzania (Tanganyika) became independent within about 18 months of each other, so differences in their outcomes cannot simply be blamed on timing. Ask learners to hold two questions in mind: what did leaders inherit, and what choices did they make?

slides.title: Why compare the Congo and Tanzania?
slides.lead: Two states, independent at almost the same moment, that reached very different destinations.

blocks.text: CAPS asks us to weigh the **successes and challenges** of independence. Comparing the Congo and Tanzania makes this concrete: one became a byword for instability and foreign interference, the other for stability, unity and idealistic but economically difficult reform.

items: Both were large territories with many ethnic and language groups.
Both faced the challenge of building a nation from a colonial state.
Their contrasting outcomes let us test what really shaped independent Africa - inheritance, leadership, ideology and outside powers.

slides.notes: Set up the comparison explicitly as an essay skill. A strong Grade 12 answer does not just narrate each country; it isolates variables (colonial inheritance, quality of leadership, ideological choices, Cold War pressure) and explains why the same starting point produced different results. Encourage learners to build a comparison table as the next slides unfold.

slides.title: The Congo under Belgian rule
slides.lead: A vast, resource-rich colony deliberately kept unprepared for self-government.

blocks.text: The Congo was first ruled as the personal property of King **Leopold II** of Belgium and then, from 1908, as the Belgian Congo. Colonial rule was brutally extractive - rich in rubber, copper and minerals - yet Belgium invested almost nothing in training Congolese leaders. At independence there were only a handful of Congolese university graduates and no African army officers.

items: The economy was built around exporting minerals, especially from copper-rich Katanga.
Belgium suppressed political activity until the last few years before independence.
Independence in June 1960 came suddenly, with little preparation.

media.alt: A map outline of central Africa with a mineral-rich southern region shaded, and a mine headgear symbol.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 160' role='img'><rect width='220' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><path d='M60 30 q50 -6 66 20 q16 22 6 44 q10 26 -14 40 q-28 12 -52 -4 q-24 -10 -22 -38 q-10 -22 4 -42 q14 -22 34 -20 Z' fill='#7a8b5a'/><rect x='84' y='96' width='44' height='30' fill='#c8a24a' opacity='0.85'/><path d='M96 96 l10 -14 l10 14 Z' fill='#5c4a2a'/><rect x='104' y='96' width='4' height='30' fill='#5c4a2a'/></svg>

slides.notes: Explain 'extractive' as a system designed to remove wealth, not to develop the colony. The key analytical point is the *inheritance*: the Congo entered independence with enormous mineral wealth but almost no trained administrators, officers or experienced politicians - a recipe for instability. [SME] The 'handful of graduates' claim is a standard textbook point; verify the precise figure a marker would expect (commonly cited as around a dozen to seventeen).

slides.title: The Congo Crisis
slides.lead: Within days of independence the new state fell into mutiny, secession and Cold War intervention.

blocks.text: At independence on 30 June 1960, **Patrice Lumumba** became Prime Minister and Joseph Kasavubu President. Almost immediately the army mutinied, Belgium sent in troops, and the mineral-rich province of **Katanga** declared secession under Moise Tshombe. Lumumba appealed to the United Nations and then the Soviet Union for help, which alarmed Western governments.

items: The UN deployed a large peacekeeping force but would not crush the Katangan secession.
Lumumba was dismissed, arrested and murdered in January 1961, with Belgian and Katangan involvement.
The crisis became a Cold War flashpoint that shaped the Congo for decades.

media.alt: A country outline split by a jagged red line, with two opposing arrows pushing against it, symbolising secession and outside pressure.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 160' role='img'><rect width='220' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><path d='M60 28 q54 -4 68 24 q14 24 2 46 q10 26 -18 40 q-30 10 -54 -8 q-22 -12 -18 -40 q-10 -22 6 -42 q14 -22 34 -20 Z' fill='#c8b89a'/><path d='M118 40 l-14 30 l16 8 l-12 32' fill='none' stroke='#8a1f1f' stroke-width='5' stroke-linejoin='round'/><path d='M18 90 h26 l-8 -8 m8 8 l-8 8' fill='none' stroke='#2f5233' stroke-width='4'/><path d='M202 90 h-26 l8 -8 m-8 8 l8 8' fill='none' stroke='#2f5233' stroke-width='4'/></svg>

slides.notes: This is the analytical heart of the Congo case. Draw out three forces tearing the state apart: internal division (army mutiny, ethnic and regional tension), economic stakes (Katanga's copper), and Cold War rivalry (the West's fear that Lumumba would turn to Moscow). Handle Lumumba's murder with care and honesty: he was killed with Belgian and Katangan complicity, and many historians also point to Western intelligence involvement. [SME] The extent of US/CIA responsibility for Lumumba's death is historically debated; present it as strongly alleged rather than settled fact.

slides.eyebrow: Case study: the Congo - the long aftermath
slides.title: Mobutu, dictatorship and decline
slides.lead: Instability opened the door to a 32-year dictatorship.

blocks.text: In 1965 army commander **Joseph Mobutu** (later Mobutu Sese Seko) seized power in a coup, backed by Western governments who valued him as a Cold War ally. He renamed the country **Zaire** in 1971, banned opposition, and ruled until 1997. His regime became notorious for corruption and self-enrichment while much of the population grew poorer - a pattern sometimes called 'kleptocracy'.

items: Mobutu concentrated power in a one-party state and a cult of personality.
Foreign backing kept him in office despite economic collapse.
His fall in 1997 was followed by further war in the region.

media.alt: A downward-trending line over a background of banknote shapes, symbolising economic decline amid corruption.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 160' role='img'><rect width='220' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><g fill='#dcd3c2'><rect x='24' y='40' width='34' height='20' rx='2'/><rect x='150' y='104' width='34' height='20' rx='2'/></g><polyline points='24,44 70,64 104,58 150,96 200,124' fill='none' stroke='#8a1f1f' stroke-width='5' stroke-linecap='round' stroke-linejoin='round'/><path d='M200 124 l-14 -2 m14 2 l-2 -14' fill='none' stroke='#8a1f1f' stroke-width='5' stroke-linecap='round'/></svg>

slides.notes: The Congo case shows how a chaotic decolonisation and outside interference can entrench authoritarian rule. Make the Cold War logic explicit: the West tolerated Mobutu's corruption because he was anti-communist and protected access to minerals. This is the clearest 'challenge' outcome of independence - formal sovereignty without genuine development or accountable government.

slides.title: From Tanganyika to Tanzania
slides.lead: A more peaceful road to independence, and a deliberate project of national unity.

blocks.text: Tanganyika, a former German then British-administered territory, won independence peacefully in December 1961 under **Julius Nyerere** and his party, TANU (the Tanganyika African National Union). In 1964 Tanganyika united with the islands of **Zanzibar** to form the United Republic of **Tanzania**. Nyerere, known as *Mwalimu* ('teacher'), promoted national unity above ethnic loyalty and made Swahili a unifying national language.

items: Independence came through negotiation, not war, giving a more stable start.
A shared national language helped bind together more than 100 ethnic groups.
Nyerere built a one-party state, but one focused on unity and social goals rather than personal enrichment.

media.alt: Two shapes, a mainland and an island, joined by a link, beneath a rising sun.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 160' role='img'><rect width='220' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><circle cx='110' cy='40' r='24' fill='#e8a13a'/><path d='M40 80 q40 -10 70 6 q10 30 -14 46 q-40 10 -58 -12 q-14 -18 2 -40 Z' fill='#2f5233'/><ellipse cx='168' cy='104' rx='22' ry='16' fill='#3f6b45'/><rect x='108' y='96' width='40' height='6' fill='#8a1f1f'/></svg>

slides.notes: Contrast with the Congo at every step: peaceful transfer of power, a single dominant nationalist party with a coherent programme, and above all a leader who prioritised nation-building over personal power. Explain the deliberate use of Swahili and a national identity to defuse the ethnic fragmentation that helped destroy the Congo. Introduce Nyerere's reputation for personal integrity as a variable that mattered.

slides.title: Ujamaa and the Arusha Declaration
slides.lead: In 1967 Nyerere set out a distinctive path: African socialism and self-reliance.

blocks.text: We have been oppressed a great deal, we have been exploited a great deal and we have been disregarded a great deal. It is our weakness that has led to our being oppressed, exploited and disregarded.

The **Arusha Declaration** committed Tanzania to **ujamaa** (a Swahili word meaning 'familyhood') - a home-grown socialism built on cooperation, equality and self-reliance rather than dependence on foreign capital. The state nationalised key industries and banks, and encouraged people to live and farm together in cooperative *ujamaa* villages.

items: Leaders were expected to live modestly and not enrich themselves.
Investment was directed to rural areas, schools and clinics.
The goal was development that Tanzanians controlled themselves.

slides.notes: Ujamaa is the clearest example of 'African socialism' from the ideas slide, so explicitly link back. Explain the appeal: after colonial exploitation, self-reliance was a way to avoid new economic dependence. Preview the tension on the next slide - the *values* were admired worldwide, but the *villagisation* programme, especially when it became coercive in the 1970s, disrupted farming and did not deliver the hoped-for growth.

slides.eyebrow: Case study: Tanzania - weighing it up
slides.title: Tanzania: successes and challenges

items: Political stability and national unity with little ethnic conflict.
Major gains in literacy, primary schooling and basic health care.
A widely respected leader seen as honest and principled.
A strong, independent voice in African and world affairs, and support for liberation movements including the ANC.
The economy grew slowly and remained dependent on foreign aid.
Forced 'villagisation' disrupted farming and angered many rural people.
State-run industries and farms were often inefficient.
The single-party state limited political freedom and debate.

slides.notes: Model the balanced judgement CAPS rewards. Nyerere himself later admitted that ujamaa had failed economically, while defending its social gains. The key insight: independence delivered dignity, unity and social progress in Tanzania, but not the economic transformation people hoped for. Have learners note that 'success' and 'failure' are not simple opposites - a country can succeed on some measures and struggle on others.

slides.title: Two paths compared
slides.lead: Same era, similar inheritance of ethnic diversity - very different outcomes.

blocks.heading: Leadership
blocks.text: Congo: a fragile, contested leadership overthrown by force. Tanzania: a stable, unifying leader focused on nation-building.

blocks.heading: Outside interference
blocks.text: Congo: heavy Belgian and Cold War intervention. Tanzania: a more peaceful transfer and greater room to choose its own path.

blocks.heading: Ideology and economy
blocks.text: Congo: kleptocracy and decline. Tanzania: idealistic socialism, strong social gains but weak economic growth.

blocks.heading: The lesson
blocks.text: Outcomes were shaped by inheritance, leadership choices, ideology and the Cold War - not by independence alone.

slides.notes: This slide is the comparison table learners should be able to reproduce and argue from. Push them beyond description: which factor mattered most? A good answer might argue that leadership and outside interference were decisive, since both countries inherited similar ethnic complexity yet diverged sharply. Invite disagreement - historians weight these factors differently.

slides.title: Successes and challenges of independence

items: The end of colonial rule and the recovery of African sovereignty and dignity.
New states, flags, national languages and a shared African identity.
The OAU gave Africa a united voice and backed liberation, including in South Africa.
Expansion of education and health services in many countries.
Colonial borders forced many peoples into single states, fuelling conflict.
Economies stayed dependent on exporting raw materials.
Cold War rivalry propped up dictators and stoked civil wars.
One-party states, coups and corruption weakened democracy in many places.

slides.notes: Zoom out from the two case studies to the continent. Remind learners that the arbitrary borders drawn at the 1884-85 Berlin Conference were inherited almost unchanged - a structural challenge no leader chose. Encourage a mature verdict: independence was a real and necessary achievement, but political freedom did not automatically bring economic freedom or stable democracy. This nuance is exactly what FET source-based and essay questions reward.

slides.title: What we learned
slides.lead: Independence was a beginning, not an ending - the real test was what each new state built next.

blocks.text: Decolonisation swept Africa in the 1950s and 1960s, driven by nationalism and Pan-Africanism. The **Congo** shows how weak inheritance and Cold War interference produced crisis and dictatorship, while **Tanzania** shows how unifying leadership and the ideals of *ujamaa* delivered stability and social progress but limited economic growth. Together they let us judge the successes and challenges of independent Africa with balance.

blocks.question: Explain how the Cold War contributed to the crisis in the Congo after independence in 1960.
blocks.label: Q1

blocks.question: Using the example of Tanzania, discuss the successes and challenges of ujamaa and the Arusha Declaration.
blocks.label: Q2

blocks.question: 'The outcomes of independence were shaped more by leadership than by anything a country inherited.' Compare the Congo and Tanzania to argue for or against this statement.
blocks.label: Q3

slides.notes: Use the three questions to cover CAPS assessment styles: Q1 explanation/cause, Q2 evaluation of one case, Q3 comparative essay argument. Encourage learners to use evidence from both case studies in Q3 and to reach a clear, defended judgement rather than sitting on the fence. Close by linking back to South Africa: Tanzania and the OAU actively supported the anti-apartheid struggle, so 'Independent Africa' is part of South Africa's own story.
